mappings.rationaleAI: Classical mechanics is the direct subject of outline Section A, covering Newtonian, Lagrangian, and Hamiltonian formulations; the article explicitly covers statics, kinematics, dynamics (Newton's laws), energy conservation, and Lagrange's and Hamilton's equations (A.6.d).

Force is defined as the central concept of classical mechanics (A.1), covered explicitly in statics/friction (A.2), and as the driver of kinetics including Newton's second and third laws (A.3.b.i); torque as the rotational analogue also connects to A.6.

Newton's three laws are the explicit content of outline A.3.b.i (law of inertia, law of force, action-reaction), and the article's coverage of impulse, momentum, and conservation (A.3.c, A.3.f) is a direct match.

Motion covers kinematics (A.3.a) — velocity, acceleration, rotation about a fixed axis, circular motion, simple harmonic motion, and relative motion — all of which are the explicit sub-paths of A.3.a in the outline.

Momentum directly covers the conservation of momentum (A.3.f), impulse and momentum (A.3.c), and elastic and inelastic collisions in the centre-of-mass system (A.4.a), all high-priority outline paths.

Energy maps directly to outline A.3.e (energy concept and conservation, forms and transformations, mass-energy equivalence) and A.3.d (work and power), covering all the specified sub-paths comprehensively.

Gravity covers Newton's law of universal gravitation (A.3.b.v), governs orbital mechanics and celestial mechanics broadly (Section B), and Einstein's general relativity reinterpretation is relevant to both B and C.

Directly addresses Section C (relativistic mechanics): special relativity foundations (C.1), relativistic kinematics including time dilation and length contraction (C.2), and the gravitational-inertial mass relationship (C.3); mass-energy equivalence covers A.3.e.iii.

Einstein's special and general relativity theories constitute the content of Section C; the article covers the postulates, kinematics, and mass-energy equivalence central to relativistic mechanics (C.1–C.3).

Wave covers vibration properties including natural, damped, and forced vibrations (E.1), types of vibrational waves and modes of propagation (E.3), and material behaviour under vibration (E.4), aligning with outline Section E.

Gas is a fluid governed by ideal gas laws and kinetic theory, directly relevant to fluid mechanics and gas dynamics (F.1 general properties, F.3 fluid in motion including compressible flow at F.3.d); turbulence and viscosity also match F.3.b–c.

Liquid covers surface tension, viscosity, buoyancy, and incompressible flow — the key properties addressed in fluid statics (F.2) and hydrodynamics (F.3); liquids are the canonical fluid studied in fluid mechanics.

Newton is the founder of classical mechanics (Principia Mathematica), directly associated with Newton's laws (A.3.b.i), law of universal gravitation (A.3.b.v), and the history of celestial mechanics (B.1).
Galileo's experimental work on free fall (kinematics, A.3.a), inertia (A.3.b.i), and projectile motion (A.3.b.iii) are foundational contributions to the mechanics covered in outline Section A.

Archimedes established foundational principles of statics (lever and center of gravity, A.2) and hydrostatics including the principle of buoyancy (F.2, F.2.b) — two uncovered or lightly covered areas in the outline.

Steel's high elastic modulus and yield/fracture strength make it the paradigmatic material for stress-strain relationships (D.1), elastic constants (D.3), and yield strength and fracture (G.3) in the outline's sections on elasticity and rheology.

Thermodynamics' first law (energy conservation) underpins outline A.3.e.i, and thermodynamic properties of fluids are essential to fluid mechanics (F.1 general properties of fluids including thermodynamic properties).

Mechanical engineering explicitly encompasses mechanics, structural analysis, dynamics, thermodynamics, and computational fluid dynamics, spanning the entire outline's scope from classical mechanics (A) through elasticity (D), vibrations (E), and fluid mechanics (F).

Euler made foundational contributions to fluid dynamics (Euler equations, F.3), analytical mechanics including the Lagrangian/Hamiltonian framework Euler helped develop (A.6.d), and celestial mechanics (B.1), covering three of the outline's major divisions.

Noether's theorem establishes the direct link between continuous symmetry and conservation laws, providing the deep theoretical basis for conservation of momentum (A.3.f) and impulse-momentum relations (A.3.c) in classical mechanics.

Mass is one of the fundamental parameters of classical mechanics (A.1), central to mass-energy equivalence (A.3.e.iii), and the equivalence of gravitational and inertial mass is explicitly the subject of outline path C.3.
Natural satellites orbit under gravitational perturbations, directly illustrating the two-body problem and orbital deviations (B.2) and aspects of the three-body problem (B.3) in celestial mechanics.

Engines convert energy forms into mechanical energy (work and power, A.3.d) and represent the most prevalent application of energy transformation chains (A.3.e.ii); heat engines are directly governed by thermodynamic-mechanical principles.
